title: Iran War Energy Disruptions Threaten African and Global Food Security Through Fertilizer Shortages
summary: The Strait of Hormuz closure and Iran war energy disruptions threaten global food security as fertilizer production costs surge, with African nations among the most vulnerable to agricultural input shortages.
contents:
## Energy-Food Security Nexus in Iran War

### Fertilizer Crisis
Global fertilizer production faces severe disruption from the Strait of Hormuz closure, which has constrained approximately 20% of global oil and gas supplies. Natural gas serves as the primary feedstock for nitrogen-based fertilizers essential to global agriculture.

### African Vulnerability
African nations face disproportionate exposure to fertilizer price increases. The continent's dependence on imported agricultural inputs, combined with limited purchasing power buffers, creates acute food security risks.

### India Price Impact
India, another heavily affected nation, raised commercial cooking gas and jet fuel prices on May 1 as supply pressures mounted. As the world's most populous nation, India's 60% dependence on imported LPG exemplifies the cascading effects of the Hormuz closure.

### Energy Cost Cascade
Brent crude at approximately $110 per barrel — reflecting a 4.5% weekly gain — translates directly into higher transportation, manufacturing, and agricultural input costs across developing economies.

### Strategic Implications
The Iran war's economic ripple effects extend far beyond the Middle East, threatening food security in Africa and South Asia while elevating the urgency of diplomatic resolution. China's elevation of Hormuz to Trump-Xi summit priority reflects this global economic dimension.
